text: I will reiterate what I've said in some previous comments... Bare minimum, any prospective World Cup host now need to have: - 14-20 host stadiums - Each of those stadiums needs at least 40,000 seats - At least 2-4 of those stadiums need at least 60,000 seats to be able to host matches from the Quarterfinals onward - At least 1 of those stadiums needs at least 80,000 seats to host the Opening Ceremony and the Final Eden Park in New Zealand has a capacity of 50,000. Every other stadium in New Zealand has less than 40,000. In fact, the four other major stadiums they have are all between 30,000-35,000. No other stadium in OFC comes remotely close. Aloha Stadium in Hawaii also has 50,000. California has maybe 7 total stadiums that would fit the bill. One in Oregon, three in Washington. In other words, to make this even remotely feasible, either New Zealand would have to upgrade at least one or two of their existing stadiums to meet FIFA requirements, and/or construct a brand new larger capacity stadium, and/or another OFC nation needs to construct a stadium that meets FIFA's requirements, and/or Hawaii/California/Oregon/Washington would need to construct one or two brand new larger capacity stadium(s)/upgrade a smaller stadium to meet FIFA requirements. 2038 is certainly far enough away that if this was a serious endeavor, that it could be done, but it would still be a massive undertaking. I just don't see OFC ever having the finances or the resources to ever host a World Cup within our lifetime.
label: Prediction

text: England has had a number of close shaves when they were good enough to do it. I think that's largely what bringing in Teuchel is about: their FA is doing their damnedest not to repeat that experience when they have Bellingham, Rice, Saka, Rogers, Palmer, and Kane still has his scoring boots on. On a much lower level, the US is not going to win the World Cup and probably never will, but they shat the bed big-time in the last Copa America, and I think they're trying to do the same thing with Poch -- rescue a (relative) golden generation that is threatening to go by the wayside without doing anything remarkable. I'm not entirely certain that either of these experiments is likely to yield results, by the way: the most successful international cup managers of the last decade or so are Didier Duchamps and Gareth Southgate. Notably, neither of them has show any interest in playing beautiful, progressive football. I think there's an argument to be made that beautiful, progressive football is not the best way to win on the international stage, and that England's "failures" of the last few years more or less come down to bad luck, not bad management.
label: Prediction

text: Unfortunately, it's the requirements for hosting a World Cup that limits which nations are capable of solo hosting now. 14-20 stadiums, each with at least 40,000 capacity, plus at least 2-4 of which with a minimum of 60,000 to host matches beyond the Round of 16, plus at least one with a minimum capacity of 80,000 to host both the Opening Ceremony and the Final, plus all the other logistical infrastructure around all the hosting venues such as travel and accommodation, training facilities, etc. The USA can basically handle all of that on its own with minimal additional costs but brought in Mexico and Canada into its initial bid to beat Morocco in order to host in 2026 (but won't have to in 2038 given host rotation rules). Aside from the USA, there's probably only the so called big five European countries (England, Spain, Italy, Germany, France) that could also solo host a World Cup now, and beyond that, probably only South Africa (with a fair amount of upgrades across the board) and China could currently handle solo hosting a World Cup. As such, co-hosting gigs between at least two or three nations is going to become far more common place purely out of necessity, as even just having 14 stadiums that meet all of FIFA's requirements is a steep ask for any one nation in most cases.
label: Prediction

text: It's the hosting rotation. Once (a) nation(s) are selected to host a World Cup, the confederation(s) they are within are ineligible to bid for the next two editions thereafter. CONCACAF in 2026, CONMEBOL, UEFA and CAF in 2030, AFC in 2034, so the only confederations eligible to let their nations bid for 2038 would be CONCACAF and OFC. Given the logistics, finances, and infrastructure required to host a World Cup, the USA are the only CONCACAF nation that can solo host a World Cup, and so if any other nations in CONCACAF were to host, they would have to do so as co-hosts with the USA. In OFC, none of the nations therein, not even New Zealand, come close to meeting requirements to host a World Cup, so a lot of investments would need to be made to allow them to host. Conceivably, a joint bid with the USA is possible but would present a lot of challenges.
label: Analysis/Explanation
notes: Another difficult boundary between majority of the text being prediction but spirit of the text is analytic in nature

text: Well, at the moment, I cannot even imagine something being better than that. I'll tell you what happened in 2022. Christiano Ronaldo had an interview with Piers Morgan. And in the interview, Morgan asks him, imagine its the World Cup Final ... Portugal vs Argentina. You vs Lionel Messi. The score is 2-2. And you kick the winning goal. How does that feel?? This was the DREAM scenario. And what actually happened. World Cup final. Messi has already scored a penalty. But France is back in the game. The score is 2-2. I'm beginning to think, maybe this will NOT happen for Messi after all. We've been here before. Messi has lost in the world cup final before. Maybe I should be happy with him just winning the Golden Boot. The momentum is with France. We go into the Extra Time. Somehow, Messi makes that run and is standing where the ball would bounce off the goal keeper. But the ball comes on his right foot instead of his left. Ofcourse he is not going to kick the winning goal with his right foot, right? No, he does. He gets you that third goal. That winning goal. That Pierce Morgan's dream goal. I go running from my drawing room to my bed room .... shouting "IT HAPPENED!!! IT HAPPENED!!!" And i cry ... the tears of joy. And then someone shoots the ball at an Argentina's face. He instinctively goes to defend his face with his elbow. But he's standing in the penalty box. The ref blows his whistle and points at the SPOT. Martinez, the goalie guesses the right direction. But Mbappe's kick is too strong. Its a GOAL again. Its 3-3. France is back in the game. And I cry again. Maybe its not going to happen for Messi after all. The momentum has gone the other way again. I don't know Messi. He does not speak English. I have never understood what he talks, how he thinks. And still I'm crying for him ... twice. And then the penalty shootouts. I've seen Messi miss a penlaty, LIVE, in the staduim, for Argentina in a Cup final before. But he makes it this time. But so does Mbappe. And I'm now thinking - its OK. Messi did his job. Nothing more can be done about this. But his team mates carry him through to the other side. Phew!!! Finally, I see Messi lift the World Cup. From the BEST GAME ever ... to the WORST GAME ever ... to the BEST GAME ever ... to the WORST GAME ever ... to the BEST GAME EVERRRRRRR The last EVERRRRR has to be in all CAPS. Because what will beat this? Can you even imagine? I cannot. First of all, forget the drama ... You'd never have a player like Messi, who you'd be rooting for so hard. And that too for his first WORLD CUP win. And that too when it was most likely going to be his last world cup.
label: Analysis/Explanation

text: I feel that a lot of it comes down to how the draw pans out. Having more teams leads to more representation but also may water down the group stage process slightly leading to more one-sides games. There is also the chance that some who have managed to scrape a win will end up going through to the Last 32. For me, more knockout football is always a good thing and I am glad that they have kept the groups of 4 to keep the final round of matches interesting. You would imagine the European heavyweights will remain the same with Spain and France being particularly strong. Argentina are certainly the form team of South America at the moment but Brazil May find a little rejuvenation under a different manager and Colombia are exciting if they can find some consistency. Japan have performed well in the last few tournaments and are probably the strongest of the Asian teams in terms of having a wider number of players playing regularly at a high level. Contenders from Africa are more wide open but I would be hoping that at least one or two can put it together when it really matters to get through to the later knockout stages at least. I am certainly excited about the prospect of it being held in the USA as 1994 was the first World Cup that I really remember and I have fond memories to look back on.
label: Prediction

text: I don’t think there’s going to be any less expensive RTBs. I think many people are unaware they even exist yet because the tournament is over a year away. If you KNOW you want to go to that specific game and you’re visiting during that time period it may be worth just getting the RTB. The only other option would maybe be the lottery, which won’t guarantee you can get tickets to that game, and the second hand market. I’ll also add that in principle I am against the RTB because it’s clearly a money grab. Additionally if you try to sell your RTB on the marketplace you have to pay fifa another 5% of the sale. But if you’re wanting to guarantee the experience, I guess that’s the game we play. $250 doesn’t sound horrible. Also keep in mind that when you’re converting to crypto it’s one a 1:1. I’d say that $250 is probably more like $275-$295 USD and it’s $250 in cryptocurrency. And the crypto “bank” takes a % fee from your conversion so it will likely be $300 USD. Probably more info than you wanted but when I bought our RTBs I had to do the transfer twice because I didn’t transfer enough the first time.
label: Prediction

text: Olympic sports are notoriously underfunded in many countries and athletes often have to find their own funding. A tournament that only takes place once every 4 years isn’t going to bring big investments. Expanding it to 48 teams also makes it a lot harder for countries to host, and hosting actually would be a great way for the sport to gain more popularity locally. Now it will be limited to a handful of countries hosting. Grassroots development is the only way for the sport to grow but that’s easier said than done in countries that don’t have big budgets. There are only a handful of professional leagues worldwide and none of them are profitable as a whole (some individual teams are). The men’s WC only expanded to 32 teams in 1998 when it already was the most popular sport on the planet for decades. And them expanding to 48 is ridiculous as well. It would be better for the WWC to grow at its own pace and to have a smaller but more competitive WWC.
label: Analysis/Explanation
